paragraphs.body: Throughout much of my life, I wouldn’t consider myself a writer to any degree, whether that be fiction, nonfiction, or an essay of sorts. Although, recently, I have felt more comfortable with writing to the point where I might even try to do so in my free time. I applied the topics learned from this class both to writing and visual art. For some context, I am a visual artist at heart and always will be. I interpret the world around me and draw it on a piece of paper in a sketchbook, on a tablet, or sometimes in extreme cases a wall or a desk. I’m mostly a character designer though, I don’t really draw landscapes unless it’s for study reasons. Prior to this class, I didn’t really put much thought into a design for a character. I would tell myself, “I want to create a character!” then I would just draw that character. Never really consider who really that character is supposed to be, or what I’m trying to say with that design. Looking back, it’d be hard to read what that character’s visual description would be outside of a person with hair wearing clothes or a monster that is surely enough made up of flesh and bones. This class help bridge the gap between writing and drawing for me and made me see that the two aren’t so different after all.

annotations.comment: Essays are nonfiction! But I like this opening and am glad you’re enjoying writing a bit more.

paragraphs.body: When the class covered character, I was more inclined to read more thoroughly through the “Bird by Bird” chapter on it and take mental and physical notes. I was mostly translating what Anne Lamott was saying about character into ways that I can grasp and use for my own work outside of class. What she had to say was really nuance, and things I had not necessarily considered prior before this reading. This passage from the chapter on character really stuck out to me,

“Now, you also want to ask yourself how they stand, what they carry in their pockets or purses, what happens in their faces and to their posture when they are thinking, or bored, or afraid. Whom would they have voted for last time? Why should we care about them anyway? What would be the first thing they stopped doing if they found out they had six months to live? Would they start smoking again? Would they keep flossing?” (Lamott, 42)

I liked this passage so much I literally stuck a sticky note on my wall with a paraphrased version of this passage written on it to use as a reminder for when I draw a new character design. The reason why this passage sticks out to me is because it can be applied to any form of art where characters are prevalent: a painting, a song, a game, an animation, etc. For me, that’s what makes great creative advice. I firmly believe that good art advice can be reinterpreted and applied across many mediums. When writing characters for this class, or when designing characters in my own work, I ask myself questions like that. Simply asking, “why?” is what I took from Lamott’s chapter on character. Why is this character angry and who are they angry towards? Has this character experienced great pain caused by the person they’re angry at? Why did this person put them through so much pain? Is this character the antagonist of the story? Bringing it back to character design; Why does this character have so much gold in their outfit? Are they an affluent ruler and want to display that power through excessive amounts of gold? Or are they perhaps a thief and want to proudly show off the things they’ve stolen? If they’re a thief, then who do they steal it from? These are the questions I ask myself now during the “sketching phase” of writing, in where I just drop-down bullet points on aspects of the story, and the literal sketching phase of drawing. The questions of why and who are powerful ones, and that was my biggest takeaway from this chapter.

annotations.comment: That’s great! And I love this passage as well.

paragraphs.body: I wanted to include my passion for visual design in the last two paragraphs because I never really considered how fictional writing practices can go hand in hand with visual design. It really sparked me to push myself more, both in this class and for my own work, and I just really wanted to state that in this paper.

Going on to other classmates’ work I gained a lot from how they wrote their characters as well. The story that I really learned from was ’s “Stasis”. This story stuck out to me in terms of character because it really showed me that less is more and subtly is key to creating much more interesting characters. Especially when writing characters who have a strong relationship, whether that be close friends or significant others. Giving characters unique quirks to how they talk depending on who really sells that idea of a living character, and if they’re friends/partners how they refer to each other. In “Stasis,” the main character Stacey refers to her friend Sydney as “Syd.” I think small details like that really add oomph for creating richer characters. Sure, they’re small compared to other aspects of the story, but they add up and surely lessen the work the writer must write to sell certain ideas about a character. It was quite the opposite in Elizabeth McCraken’s short story “It’s Not You.” The story is quite blunt about all the details about the unnamed woman serving as the main character. With her being the narrator told me a lot about her, and the story didn’t even have to weirdly abrupt itself to tell me that she is a blunt woman going through heartbreak, it simply just did it. It allowed for the plot’s messaging to be a little more interpretative rather than the character themself, so it overall created a nice balance.

annotations.comment: I think most very good characterization does this — it just is, rather than asserting.

paragraphs.body: To focus more on the craft of writing, I was mostly dreading it when this class started. Not having an English teacher for two years of high school, and the other times I did have one it not being the best experience either, partly to blame my rambunctious younger self, has built up a lot of anxiety for me when faced with that blank document on Word or Google Docs. I felt as if I was missing the ability to put ideas into words into sentences. I would literally freeze and it got so bad that I had to lay on my bed, tell my girlfriend the thoughts I had rolling in my head, then she would write them down into sentences for me; as if I was in those therapy sessions where the patient lays on one of those comfy looking leather seats and tells the therapist their inner most thoughts. Looking back though, most papers I wrote in high school and college were argumentative or persuasive essays, in where I have to site information and be sure to know the topic of discussion well. So, it felt I had to get anything and everything about the paper correct, or I would fail. However, I think this class broke that stigma for me and I feel much more confident about the craft of writing. It helped me to be able to tie writing with something I’m already familiar with, and I think that bridge was vital for me to become comfortable trying to write. The workshops also helped me see that everyone is also figuring their stories out, and that nobody has something that I’m missing like I’ve thought for many years.

paragraphs.body: Overall, this class has really boosted my confidence in storytelling, whether it be for writing or drawing characters. I have never felt less anxious looking at a blank Word or Google doc paper before, and I certainly feel comfortable with the idea that the first draft is never the best draft. It’s just the foundation for something greater.

annotations.comment: Please keep writing and creating, Adeline! Thank you for your hard work this term.
